n: The Wisdom of Solomon Pyramid, St Anne's Limehouse
hook: A stranded Hawksmoor pyramid sulking in a Limehouse churchyard
facts: St Anne's was consecrated 1730, one of Hawksmoor's Commission churches
The Portland-stone pyramid, c.1730, bears the carving 'The Wisdom of Solomon'
It was likely meant to crown a corner of the church but never made it up
Grade I church, Grade II pyramid
legend: Psychogeographers link the pyramid to Hawksmoor occult ley-lines across the East End
w: Hawksmoor meant two stone pyramids to crown St Anne's east corners. One vanished; the other got marooned in the churchyard, squat and inscrutable, carved 'The Wisdom of Solomon'. Nobody quite knows why. Psychogeographers read ley-lines into it. Most likely just a leftover. A baroque shrug in Portland stone.

hook: A modern church built inside the bombed shell of a Hawksmoor giant
facts: Hawksmoor church, built 1715-29 in Portland stone
Gutted by a 1941 Blitz bomb; only walls and pepper-pot towers survived
Worship ran for years in a prefab inside the ruin, 'St George in the Ruins'
A modern interior was built within the old shell in 1964
w: A 1941 bomb gutted Hawksmoor's giant on Cannon Street Road, leaving only walls and the squat pepper-pot towers. For seventeen years a prefab chapel huddled in the ruin, 'St George in the Ruins'. In 1964 they slotted a modern church inside the old shell. A building wearing its own corpse like a coat.

hook: A Commonwealth-era chapel the East India Company built for its sailors
facts: Poplar Chapel begun 1652-54, paid for by the East India Company
One of very few churches surviving from the Cromwellian Commonwealth
Gothic-clad in the 19th century; ship-motif details inside nod to the EIC
Deconsecrated, now a community centre; Grade II* listed
w: The East India Company built this chapel for its Blackwall shipwrights and sailors in the 1650s, one of the rare churches raised under Cromwell's godly republic. Later centuries wrapped it in Gothic stone, but inside lurk ship motifs and Company arms. Now a community hall. A merchant empire's parish church, hidden in plain sight.

n: St Dunstan and All Saints, Stepney
hook: The Saxon-rooted 'mother church of the East End', with an 11th-century carved rood
facts: Founded or refounded around AD 952, traditionally by St Dunstan himself
Once served all of Middlesex east of the City wall
An early-11th-century Anglo-Saxon Crucifixion relief sits behind the altar
Known as 'the Church of the High Seas'; Grade I listed
legend: Said to be founded by St Dunstan, patron of bellringers and metalworkers
w: Stepney's mother church, traceable to around 952 and once parish to everything east of the City wall. Behind the altar hangs a carved Saxon rood from the early 1000s, one of only two such survivals in London. Sailors born at sea were registered here, hence 'the Church of the High Seas'. Older than the East End itself.

hook: Hackney's oldest building, a lone medieval tower with a Tudor turret clock
facts: Survives from the medieval parish church, rebuilt in Kentish ragstone c.1500s
The rest of the old church was demolished in 1798 after St John's opened
Houses a turret clock thought to date from the 1570s, mostly original
Now run by the Hackney Historic Buildings Trust; open occasionally
w: When Hackney's medieval church came down in 1798, they kept the tower. It still stands in a corner of the churchyard, Kentish ragstone over rougher early stonework, with a turret clock ticking since around the 1570s, most of its gears original. Climb it on open days. The oldest thing in the borough, outliving its own church.

n: Soup Kitchen for the Jewish Poor, Brune Street
hook: A 1902 terracotta facade still reading 'Soup Kitchen for the Jewish Poor'
facts: Purpose-built 1902 by architect Lewis Solomon on then-Butler Street
Cornice reads 'Soup Kitchen For the Jewish Poor 5662 1902', with 'Way In/Way Out'
Fed over 5,000 people a week during the Great Depression
Closed 1992 after 90 years; the listed facade now fronts flats
w: Buff terracotta lettering still spells it out on a Spitalfields side street: 'Soup Kitchen For the Jewish Poor', dated 5662 in the Hebrew calendar, 1902 in ours. Doorways marked Way In and Way Out. In the Depression it dispensed bread, meat and soup to over 5,000 souls a week. Closed in 1992. Flats now, but the words remain.

hook: Memorial to 173 crushed to death on a tube shelter stairway in 1943
facts: Commemorates Britain's worst civilian disaster of WWII, 3 March 1943
173 people died in a crush on the stairs into Bethnal Green tube shelter
No bomb fell; panic on a wet, unlit staircase caused the deaths
The inverted-stairway sculpture was unveiled in 2017 after a long campaign
w: On a wet March night in 1943, a woman stumbled on the dark stairs down to Bethnal Green's tube shelter. The crowd behind kept pushing. In minutes 173 people lay dead, crushed, no bomb involved, the worst civilian disaster of the British war. Hushed up for years. A bronze inverted staircase now hangs above the spot.

hook: A working city farm on Victorian dock-mud heaped over WWII gun emplacements
facts: The name comes from mud pumped from Millwall Dock in the 1860s and dumped here
Four anti-aircraft guns stood on the high ground during the Blitz
Saved from tower-block development by locals in 1977
Now 32 acres of pasture, livestock and woodland under Canary Wharf's towers
w: The name says it: spoil and silt pumped from Millwall Dock in the 1860s, heaped over East Ferry Road into a man-made hill. Ack-ack guns crowned it in the Blitz. Locals fought off the tower blocks in 1977 and put sheep, llamas and pigs on it instead. Now a farm grazing in Canary Wharf's shadow.

hook: The last hydraulic power station on earth, once driving London's lifts by water
facts: Opened 1890 by the London Hydraulic Power Company on Wapping Wall
Pumped water through 186 miles of mains to work lifts, cranes and theatre stages
Shut at 8am on 1 July 1977, the last public hydraulic station anywhere
Grade II* listed; later ran as an arts venue and restaurant
w: London once ran on water pressure. From 1890, this Wapping Wall station pushed 33 million gallons a week through 186 miles of buried mains, raising lifts, cranes and West End stage curtains. The pumps stopped at 8am on 1 July 1977, the last public hydraulic station on the planet. Industrial sorcery, switched off.

hook: A riverside pub where Nelson kept his trysts with Lady Hamilton, they say
facts: A pub has stood at 27 Coldharbour since the early 1700s, named The Gun by 1771
Named for the cannon fired when West India Docks opened in 1802
A spy-hole on a hidden stair let smugglers watch for the Revenue men
Grade II listed; rebuilt after a 2001 fire
legend: Said to be where Nelson met Lady Emma Hamilton in an upstairs room, now 'The River Room'
w: Tucked on the riverbank at Coldharbour, this old dockers' tavern took its name from the cannon fired when West India Docks opened in 1802. Smugglers landed contraband below, watching for the Revenue through a spy-hole on a secret stair. Legend has Nelson slipping upstairs to meet Lady Hamilton. Thames lapping at the windows.

hook: Ming, Pekin and Canton Streets, all that survives of London's first Chinatown
facts: Chinese sailors from East India ships settled here from the late 1700s
Around 300 lived in the quarter at its 1930s peak, around Pennyfields and Ming Street
Bomb damage and slum clearance erased the community by the 1940s
Ming Street was renamed in 1938; Pekin and Canton Streets predate it
legend: Pulp fiction painted the quarter as a den of opium and intrigue, the 'Limehouse Blues' myth
w: London's first Chinatown grew in Limehouse from sailors off the East India ships, a few hundred strong by the 1930s, with laundries and chophouses around Pennyfields. Pulp novels turned it into an opium fantasy. Bombs and slum clearance did the rest. All that survives now: street signs reading Ming, Pekin, Canton, Nankin. Ghost names.

n: The Green Bridge, Mile End Park
hook: A grass-and-tree bridge carrying a park clean over the Mile End Road
facts: Carries Mile End Park's two halves over the busy A11
Designed by local resident-architect Piers Gough after a 1995 planning weekend
Trees and turf grow on its deck; underside painted yellow, hence 'banana bridge'
Park was built on land flattened by WWII bombing
w: Mile End Park got sliced in two by the roaring A11, so they lifted the grass over it. The Green Bridge carries trees and turf clean across the traffic, designed by local architect Piers Gough off a 1995 community weekend. Locals call it the banana bridge for its yellow belly. Walk through a park and never notice the road below.

n: Captain Cook's House Wall, Mile End Road
hook: A plaque on a bare wall where the explorer's London home once stood
facts: Cook lived at 88 Mile End Road from 1764 until his death in 1779
The house was demolished in 1959 to widen access to a car park behind
A slate GLC plaque, set 1970, marks the empty spot
Cook's wife Elizabeth outlived him here by decades
w: James Cook kept house at 88 Mile End Road from 1764 to his death in Hawaii in 1779, returning between voyages that redrew the Pacific. In 1959 the borough knocked it down to widen a car-park entrance. All that marks the great navigator's London home now: a slate plaque, screwed to a blank brick wall. Charted the world, lost his house.

hook: The exact riverbank spot of Canaletto's famous Greenwich view
facts: A small park at the southern tip of the Isle of Dogs, opened 1895
Frames the Greenwich panorama Canaletto painted in the 1750s
Marks the north mouth of the 1902 Greenwich foot tunnel
Wren reputedly admired the Royal Hospital from this bank
legend: Said to be where Christopher Wren stood to survey his Greenwich Hospital across the water
w: Stand at the Isle of Dogs' southern tip and the river frames Greenwich exactly as Canaletto painted it in the 1750s: the Queen's House threaded between Wren's twin domes, the Observatory on its hill. Wren reputedly studied his hospital from this very bank. A glazed rotunda beside you drops to the foot tunnel under the Thames.

hook: Edwardian-style hammam beneath an East End boxing temple
facts: Opened 1929 by the Duke and Duchess of York, the future George VI
Built on a former starch factory; included Turkish, Russian and slipper baths
Famous as a boxing venue from the late 1940s, hosting world title fights
One of London's last surviving public Turkish baths, with a working hammam
w: Above, the canvas where East End boys boxed for world titles since the 1940s. Below, a steam-wreathed hammam that has scrubbed Bethnal Green since 1929, opened by the future George VI on the bones of an old starch factory. One of London's last public Turkish baths. Heavyweight glory upstairs, hot marble and quiet down.
